meta.current-phase: Manual internal client provisioning parity live-validated on fresh RU and DE infrastructure and released in v2.4.24

status-summary.done: Backend, user frontend, admin frontend, bot and deploy surface already exist as an MVP. VPN topology has moved toward VPNNode/VPNRoute but routing policy is still mostly IP-based.
status-summary.in-progress: Synchronizing GRACE shared artifacts with the real codebase. Designing a policy-driven routing layer that can classify traffic by domain and IP. Designing a backend-driven manual provisioning path so operator-created configs match web-generated configs.
status-summary.blocked-or-weak: Existing split-tunneling logic is too rigid for domain-level routing. Verification is still weaker than required for autonomous changes in routing-critical code.

WS-001.name: AI Handoff and Documentation Accuracy
WS-001.goal: Keep GRACE artifacts aligned with the implemented system and planned migration path.

WS-002.name: Security and Deployment Hardening
WS-002.goal: Reduce operational risk in secrets handling, admin access and deployment defaults.

WS-003.name: VPN Topology and Provisioning Stability
WS-003.goal: Finish stabilizing VPNNode/VPNRoute-based provisioning and analytics invariants.

WS-004.name: Flexible Routing Policy Migration
WS-004.goal: Introduce a routing decision engine that supports exact-domain, wildcard-domain, DNS-bound IP and CIDR-based classification without breaking the current RU-vs-non-RU baseline.

WS-005.name: Verification and Testability
WS-005.goal: Raise routing, VPN and admin verification to a level safe for autonomous execution.

WS-006.name: Manual Internal Client Provisioning
WS-006.goal: Replace raw host-only client config generation with a backend-driven internal-access flow that produces the same working configs as the web path.

M-001.name: backend-core
M-001.purpose: Configuration, security, DB lifecycle and bootstrap primitives.

M-002.name: users
M-002.purpose: User auth, profile and Telegram-linked identity flows.

M-003.name: vpn
M-003.purpose: VPN provisioning, config generation and topology-aware client assignment.

M-004.name: billing
M-004.purpose: Plans, subscriptions, payments and webhook processing.

M-005.name: referrals
M-005.purpose: Referral codes, links and bonus accounting.

M-006.name: admin-api
M-006.purpose: Privileged analytics and operational control endpoints.

M-007.name: routing
M-007.purpose: Host-level split-tunneling, ipset/iptables coordination and routing health inspection.

M-008.name: tasks
M-008.purpose: Background scheduling and recurring maintenance jobs.

M-009.name: frontend-user
M-009.purpose: Main user UI for auth, config delivery, subscription and referrals.

M-010.name: frontend-admin
M-010.purpose: Admin UI for analytics, plans, users, servers and routing surfaces.

M-011.name: telegram-bot
M-011.purpose: Telegram bot orchestration over backend HTTP APIs.

M-012.name: deploy-surface
M-012.purpose: Install, deploy, nginx and compose surfaces that shape production behavior.

M-013.name: route-policy-resolver
M-013.purpose: Decide routing target using explicit domain rules, DNS-bound addresses, explicit CIDR rules and fallback policy.

M-014.name: domain-rule-store
M-014.purpose: Persist exact, wildcard and priority-ordered routing rules for domains and CIDRs.

M-015.name: dns-observer
M-015.purpose: Track domain-to-IP bindings from DNS resolution and maintain TTL-aware policy cache updates.

M-016.name: route-decision-api
M-016.purpose: Expose admin controls and inspection endpoints for routing policy rules and effective decisions.

M-017.name: route-sync-runtime
M-017.purpose: Translate resolved policy decisions into host-level routing sets and incremental refresh actions.

M-018.name: manual-access-policy
M-018.purpose: Grant and reuse internal non-billable access for operator-managed users without bypassing the main subscription and provisioning model.

M-019.name: manual-client-cli
M-019.purpose: Create or reuse an internal user, ensure complimentary access, provision a VPN client, and export a working config through backend CLI.

DF-001.name: Domain-first routing decision
DF-001.summary: Admin-defined domain policy is evaluated first. Exact and wildcard domain rules override generic RU IP heuristics.

DF-002.name: DNS binding to runtime route set
DF-002.summary: DNS-derived IPs for a matched domain are cached and synchronized into the runtime route set with TTL awareness.

DF-003.name: Fallback CIDR classification
DF-003.summary: If no domain policy applies, explicit CIDR rules and the RU baseline determine whether traffic stays local or exits via DE.

DF-004.name: Provisioning and config consistency
DF-004.summary: VPN provisioning and routing runtime share the same decision policy so client expectations match traffic behavior.

DF-005.name: Admin observability of policy decisions
DF-005.summary: Operators can inspect why a domain or IP was routed to RU, DE, DIRECT or DEFAULT.

DF-006.name: Manual internal client provisioning
DF-006.summary: Operator-run client creation resolves or creates an internal user, grants non-billable access, provisions a VPN client and returns the same config shape as the web flow.

DF-007.name: Config parity between web and CLI paths
DF-007.summary: Web registration and operator CLI issuance must converge on the same VPNService-based topology selection and config rendering path.

DF-008.name: Manual client idempotency and reprovision safety
DF-008.summary: Re-running manual client issuance for the same identity should reuse or explicitly reprovision state instead of creating silent duplicate active clients.

Phase-1.name: Policy model and safe compatibility layer
Phase-1.step-1: Create M-014 rule entities and schemas for exact-domain, wildcard-domain and CIDR rules.
Phase-1.step-2: Create M-013 resolver with deterministic precedence: domain exact, domain wildcard, DNS-bound IP, explicit CIDR, RU baseline, default.
Phase-1.step-3: Keep current RU-vs-non-RU behavior as fallback so rollout remains backward compatible.

Phase-2.name: Runtime synchronization and DNS binding
Phase-2.step-1: Extend M-017 runtime sync so policy changes become incremental host-level updates instead of full resets where possible.
Phase-2.step-2: Add M-015 DNS observer and bounded cache with TTL-driven expiry and refresh.
Phase-2.step-3: Introduce decision logging anchors for every policy branch.

Phase-3.name: Admin control plane and operator visibility
Phase-3.step-1: Extend M-016 with CRUD for domain rules and read-only decision inspection endpoints.
Phase-3.step-2: Expose policy and resolution state in the admin frontend without changing the user-facing product flow yet.
Phase-3.step-3: Add migration-safe analytics so legacy VPNServer-based views do not silently misrepresent routing state.

Phase-4.name: Hardening and rollout gates
Phase-4.step-1: Run routing-focused verification on exact-domain, wildcard-domain, TTL expiry and fallback branches.
Phase-4.step-2: Gate production rollout on host-level safety review for ipset or nftables updates.
Phase-4.step-3: Only after evidence is stable, make domain-aware policy the preferred routing control surface.

Phase-5.name: Manual internal access and CLI provisioning parity
Phase-5.step-1: Add an internal non-billable access model that remains visible in normal user, subscription and VPN state instead of bypassing billing and provisioning.
Phase-5.step-2: Add backend CLI orchestration that creates or resolves the internal user, ensures access, provisions the client and exports config through VPNService.
Phase-5.step-3: Convert deploy/create-client.sh into a thin wrapper over the backend CLI so manual configs no longer use raw host-only generation.
Phase-5.step-4: Verify parity between manually issued configs and web-issued configs, including idempotency and restart-safe peer persistence.

observability-notes.item: Every route decision must include a stable reason code: domain_exact, domain_wildcard, dns_bound_ip, cidr_rule, ru_baseline, default.
DNS binding refresh must emit stable logs for add, refresh, expire and conflict branches.
Runtime sync must expose whether a rule was applied incrementally, skipped, or required a full refresh.
Manual client issuance must emit stable markers for request, identity resolution, complimentary access grant, provisioning outcome and config export.

scenario.name: Known Russian domain
scenario.summary: Client requests a domain matched by an exact or wildcard RU rule. Resolver returns RU without depending on the current resolved IP.

scenario.name: Known foreign domain behind changing CDN IPs
scenario.summary: Domain rule sends traffic to DE. DNS observer keeps the changing IP set aligned so CDN drift does not collapse policy correctness.

scenario.name: Unknown domain with no explicit rule
scenario.summary: Resolver falls back to explicit CIDR policy and finally the existing RU baseline, preserving current behavior until policy coverage grows.

scenario.name: Operator creates internal client for family use
scenario.summary: Admin runs the manual client command, backend resolves or creates an internal identity, grants non-billable access, provisions a VPN client and returns a config that matches the normal user flow.

scenario.name: Repeated manual issue for the same client name
scenario.summary: CLI resolves the same internal identity and reuses or explicitly reprovisions the existing client rather than creating hidden duplicate state with a different peer or IP.

risks.item: Domain-aware routing is incomplete if DNS bindings are not captured before traffic reaches only-IP enforcement points.
CDN and shared-IP domains can cause false sharing unless rule priority and conflict handling are explicit.
Host-level rule churn can degrade stability if runtime sync is not incremental and bounded.
Admin analytics can drift from real routing behavior if legacy VPNServer assumptions remain unadjusted.
Manual client issuance will remain unreliable if deploy/create-client.sh keeps generating peers and configs outside backend topology and subscription state.
Internal non-billable access can pollute billing analytics unless complimentary access is explicitly marked and reported.
Unsafe reprovision rules could create duplicate active clients or mismatched peer state for the same internal identity.
